author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
Manichæus Promises Truth, But Does Not Make Good His Word.

title: Manichæus Promises Truth, But Does Not Make Good His Word.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: "These," he says, "are wholesome words from the perennial and living fountain; and whoever shall have heard them, and shall have first believed them, and then shall have observed the truths they set forth, shall never suffer death, but shall enjoy eternal life in glory.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: For he is to be judged truly blessed who has been instructed in this divine knowledge, by which he is made free and shall abide in everlasting life."

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: And this, as you see, is a promise of truth, but not the bestowal of it.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: And you yourselves can easily see that any errors whatever might be dressed up in this fashion, so as under cover of a showy exterior to steal in unawares into the minds of the ignorant.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Were he to say, These are pestiferous words from a poisonous fountain; and whoever shall have heard them, and shall have first believed them, and then have observed what they set forth, shall never be restored to life, but shall suffer a woful death as a criminal: for assuredly he is to be pronounced miserable who falls into this infernal error, in which he will sink so as to abide in everlasting torments;—were he to say this, he would say the truth; but instead of gaining any readers for his book, he would excite the greatest aversion in the minds of all into whose hands the book might come.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Let us then pass on to what follows; nor let us be deceived by words which may be used alike by good and bad, by learned and unlearned.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: "May the peace," he says, "of the invisible God, and the knowledge of the truth, be with the holy and beloved brethren who both believe and also yield obedience to the divine precepts."

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: For the prayer is a most amiable and commendable one.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Only we must bear in mind that these words might be used by false teachers as well as by good ones.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: So, if he said nothing more than this, all might safely read and embrace it.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Nor should I disapprove of what follows: "May also the right hand of light protect you, and deliver you from every hostile assault, and from the snares of the world."

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: In fact, I have no fault to find with the beginning of this epistle, till we come to the main subject of it.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: For I wish not to spend time on minor points.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Now, then, for this writer’s plain statement of what is to be expected from him.
